stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[a]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the entry in the first row and second column (the top-right entry) of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Writing \( A = [[a]]I + N \) with \( N = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & [[b]] \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \) and \( N^2 = 0 \), the binomial theorem gives \( A^{[[n]]} = [[a]]^{[[n]]}I + [[n]]\,[[a]]^{[[n]]-1}N \). The top-right entry is \( [[n]]\cdot[[a]]^{[[n]]-1}\cdot([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the diagonal entry \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} \), not the top-right entry.
This treats \( A \) as if its diagonal were 1, giving \( n\cdot b \).
This forgets the factor of \( [[n]] \) coming from the \( n \) equal terms.
This uses \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( [[a]]^{[[n]]-1} \) in the off-diagonal term.
This is off by \( [[b]] \) from the correct entry.
This is off by \( [[b]] \) from the correct entry.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[b]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the trace of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Since \( A \) is diagonal, \( A^{[[n]]} = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]]^{[[n]]} & 0 \\ 0 & [[b]]^{[[n]]} \end{bmatrix} \), so \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[n]]}) = [[a]]^{[[n]]} + [[b]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This raises the SUM of the diagonal entries to the \( n \)-th power instead of raising each entry first.
This multiplies the sum by \( n \) instead of raising each entry to the \( n \)-th power.
This is the determinant of \( A^{[[n]]} \), not the trace.
This multiplies each entry by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by one from the correct trace.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & [[c]] \\ [[c]] & 0 \end{bmatrix} \). What is the entry in the first row and second column of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Since \( A^2 = [[c]]^2 I \), powers of \( A \) alternate: \( A^{[[n]]} = [[c]]^{[[n]]}I \) when \( [[n]] \) is even (so the top-right entry is \( 0 \)), and \( A^{[[n]]} = [[c]]^{[[n]]-1}A \) when \( [[n]] \) is odd (so the top-right entry is \( [[c]]^{[[n]]} \)). Compactly, the top-right entry is \( [[c]]^{[[n]]}\cdot\dfrac{1-(-1)^{[[n]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value when \( n \) is odd, applied without checking the parity of \( n \) here.
This uses the DIAGONAL entry's parity formula instead of the off-diagonal one.
This linearizes the power instead of applying the alternating-parity pattern.
This is off by one power of \( c \) from the correct pattern.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by \( c \) from the correct entry.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[A11]] & [[A12]] \\ [[A21]] & [[A22]] \end{bmatrix} \), whose rows are all scalar multiples of \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \), so \( A \) has rank \( 1 \) and \( A^2 = \operatorname{tr}(A)\,A \). What is the entry in the first row and first column of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: For a rank-\(1\) matrix, \( A^2 = \operatorname{tr}(A)A \), and by induction \( A^{[[n]]} = \operatorname{tr}(A)^{[[n]]-1}A \). Here \( \operatorname{tr}(A) = [[t]] \), so the \((1,1)\) entry of \( A^{[[n]]} \) is \( [[t]]^{[[n]]-1}\cdot[[A11]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \((1,1)\) entry of \( A \) itself, without the trace-power factor.
This uses \( \operatorname{tr}(A)^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( \operatorname{tr}(A)^{[[n]]-1} \).
This omits the original \((1,1)\) entry factor, leaving only the trace power.
This uses \( \operatorname{tr}(A)^1 \) regardless of \( n \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by a factor of \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \) from the correct entry.
